quick_facts.label: What this degree is
quick_facts.value: An MS in Computer Science abroad is a postgraduate CS degree; programme design varies substantially by university, including professionally oriented coursework MS degrees and research-oriented master’s degrees.

quick_facts.label: Typical duration
quick_facts.value: Full-time master’s programmes commonly take 1–2 years. For example, Stanford reports an average full-time completion time of 1.5 years for its MSCS; the European Education Area says most full-time European master’s programmes last one or two years.

quick_facts.label: GRE and IELTS are not universal requirements
quick_facts.value: GRE policy is set by each programme: Stanford says programmes may require, make optional, or not consider it; Waterloo CS says GRE is not required. English-test requirements or exemptions are also institution-specific.

quick_facts.label: Core application materials
quick_facts.value: Applicants commonly submit academic records, a statement of purpose, CV/resume and recommendation letters; specific counts and requirements differ by programme.

quick_facts.label: Indicative cost context
quick_facts.value: Published tuition and living-cost rules are destination- and institution-specific. As broad context, the European Education Area reports non-EU tuition in Europe at roughly €1,000–€20,000 per year and typical US public-university undergraduate/graduate tuition at roughly US$10,000–$70,000 per year; applicants must use each programme’s current fee page and add living, insurance, travel and visa costs.

quick_facts.label: Post-study work is rule-dependent
quick_facts.value: A qualifying Canadian master’s programme of at least 8 months can support an application for a three-year PGWP, subject to eligibility. In the US, an eligible F-1 graduate in a qualifying STEM degree may seek a 24-month STEM OPT extension after post-completion OPT; neither route is automatic.

sections.heading: Eligibility and academic preparation
paragraphs: Admission is set by the individual university and department rather than by a single international standard. Applicants generally need a bachelor’s degree or an overseas qualification assessed as equivalent to a US bachelor’s degree; Berkeley explicitly states this expectation for graduates of accredited institutions outside the United States.

For an MS in CS, relevant prior preparation matters as much as the degree title. Waterloo’s CS graduate admissions guidance expects formal background in programming languages, data structures, operating systems, algorithms and computer organization/architecture; applicants without it may be required to take remedial courses. This makes a CS/IT/engineering degree the most direct route, while applicants from other quantitative fields should verify prerequisite coverage programme by programme.

Indian applicants should not assume that IELTS is mandatory solely because they studied in India, or that a medium-of-instruction letter will always provide a waiver. Stanford lists specified English-proficiency exemptions and otherwise requires TOEFL iBT or IELTS Academic; Waterloo notes that a university medium-of-instruction letter does not itself meet its English-language exemption requirements. Test policy must be checked against the target intake’s official departmental page.
bullets: Use the university’s country-specific equivalency guidance to check degree and grade expectations.
Map undergraduate coursework against prerequisites in programming, algorithms, systems, mathematics and architecture.
Treat GRE, TOEFL and IELTS as programme-specific requirements: submit only the tests, format and score reports the target programme currently accepts.
Check whether the programme is course-based, thesis/research-based, or offers both, because research programmes may require faculty/supervisor fit.

sections.heading: Duration and study format
paragraphs: A full-time MS in CS abroad is commonly one to two years, but completion structure varies by institution, credits, thesis option and country. Stanford describes its full-time MSCS as taking 1.5 years on average and requiring 45 units; it permits up to three years (nine non-summer quarters) to complete degree requirements. The European Education Area states that most full-time master’s programmes in Europe last one or two years.

Students should distinguish the advertised programme length from the maximum permitted time to finish, and from the academic calendar. A one-year European programme, a 16-month programme and a two-year North American programme have different tuition instalments, internship timing, visa and post-study-work implications.
bullets: Confirm the normal full-time duration, credit load and maximum completion period.
Check whether summer study is required, optional or unavailable.
For thesis/research options, confirm supervision, dissertation milestones and whether funding is available.

sections.heading: Curriculum and specialisation
paragraphs: MSCS curricula normally combine advanced CS coursework with a chosen specialisation, technical electives and, depending on the programme, a project or research component. Stanford requires an approved 45-unit academic plan and offers nine predefined MSCS specialisations. Its requirements include foundations, significant software implementation, breadth, depth and elective components.

Course titles and concentrations vary. Applicants should compare the actual module catalogue, not only the programme title: areas may include artificial intelligence, systems, theory, security, data, human-computer interaction, programming languages or interdisciplinary work in mathematics, statistics and electrical engineering. Stanford permits technically relevant upper-level courses from related departments as electives, subject to approval.
bullets: Check whether the programme is coursework-only, capstone/project-based, thesis-based or mixed.
Verify that desired subjects are offered regularly and available to master’s students.
For career plans in research or doctoral study, prioritize opportunities for thesis work, research groups and faculty supervision.
Review grading, minimum GPA and course-load rules before accepting an offer.

sections.heading: Application process
paragraphs: Prepare applications directly through each university’s graduate portal and work backwards from that programme’s deadline. Typical materials include transcripts, a CV/resume, statement of purpose, recommendation letters and any required test scores. Carnegie Mellon’s MSCS, for example, asks for transcripts, CV, statement of purpose and three recommendations, and describes its own GRE and English-test policies.

Deadlines, intakes and test policies are variable and must be treated as dated information. For example, Carnegie Mellon’s MSCS page currently describes fall-only entry and lists specific 2026 application dates, while other programmes may have different cycles. Do not generalize a deadline, score threshold or test waiver from one university to another.

For applicants educated in India, document handling is important: Berkeley requests records from each post-secondary institution and, for international credentials, transcripts and degree certificates; it gives instructions for translations and credential evaluation where needed. Ensure names, dates, degree certificates and transcripts are consistent across passport, application and testing records.
bullets: Shortlist programmes by prerequisites, curriculum, budget, location, research fit and immigration pathway—not rankings alone.
Request recommendations early and give referees a CV, draft statement and programme list.
Write a programme-specific statement linking prior preparation, interests and objectives to the target curriculum or research area.
Submit before the deadline and allow time for score reporting, transcript verification and referee uploads.
After an offer, follow the university’s instructions for deposit, final official records, financial documentation and visa paperwork.

sections.heading: Costs, funding and financial evidence
paragraphs: Costs should be budgeted as a full cost of attendance rather than tuition alone: tuition and compulsory university fees, housing, food, health insurance, local transport, books/equipment, flights, visa charges and a contingency. Broad published international-student tuition ranges are only context, not a quotation: the European Education Area reports approximately €1,000–€20,000 annually for non-EU students in Europe and approximately US$10,000–$70,000 at US public universities. Programme pages and official offer documents are the authoritative sources for an individual applicant’s amount.

Funding differs sharply between course-based and research/thesis programmes. Waterloo advises applicants to review guaranteed funding for full-time MMath and PhD students, other scholarships, tuition and living expenses; this demonstrates why applicants must confirm whether funding is guaranteed, competitive, conditional or unavailable for their specific master’s route.

Immigration financial requirements are separate from the university budget. For the UK Student visa, the current government guidance requires funds for outstanding course fees for one academic year plus maintenance of £1,529 per month in London or £1,171 per month outside London, for up to nine months, unless an exception applies. These amounts and rules can change, so applicants should recheck them immediately before applying.
bullets: Use the official programme fee page for the intended academic year; distinguish per-credit, per-term and total-programme pricing.
Ask whether scholarships, assistantships or fee waivers are available to international master’s students and whether they reduce tuition, provide a stipend, or both.
Keep a separate visa-evidence plan; an admission offer does not by itself establish that financial requirements have been met.
Convert currencies only for planning and include exchange-rate risk; pay invoices in the currency and schedule stated by the institution.

sections.heading: Options after the degree
paragraphs: Graduates may pursue software engineering and other technical roles, research and development, product/technology roles, or further study such as a PhD. The immediate options available in the country of study depend on the degree, institution, immigration status and evolving government rules—not merely on holding an MSCS.

In Canada, graduates of eligible designated learning institutions may apply for a Post-Graduation Work Permit if they meet all requirements. Canada states that, for master’s programmes of at least eight months, graduates may apply for a three-year PGWP even where the master’s programme was shorter than two years; degree graduates must also meet the stated language requirement for applications under the current rules.

In the US, practical training must be directly related to the student’s major. USCIS states that eligible F-1 students with a qualifying STEM degree may apply for a 24-month STEM OPT extension after post-completion OPT, with requirements including a qualifying employer and training plan. Applicants should verify the programme’s CIP/STEM designation, school certification and current immigration rules before relying on this route.

Returning to India after graduation is also a viable route. The degree’s value will depend on demonstrable skills, projects, internships, work authorization history and employer requirements rather than the credential title alone.
bullets: Before enrolling, check the exact degree title, CIP/STEM classification where relevant, institutional eligibility and post-study-work rules.
Use internships, capstones, research output and a portfolio/GitHub or equivalent evidence of work to strengthen employment applications.
For PhD plans, select programmes with research access, thesis options and faculty alignment rather than choosing solely on duration or location.
